standard.prompt: A message says your account will be closed in 10 minutes unless you sign in through a link. What is the safest first move?
options: Open the link immediately so the account stays active
Use the official app or website directly and check alerts there
Reply with your password so support can verify you
Forward the message to friends to ask if it is real
standard.explanation: Urgent account warnings are common phishing hooks. Going directly to the official service avoids a fake link.

standard.prompt: Which password habit is strongest for a school, game, or shopping account?
options: Use the same password everywhere so it is easy to remember
Use a unique long password or passphrase for each important account
Use your pet's name and birth year
Share your password only with close friends
standard.explanation: Unique passwords limit damage if one account is exposed.

standard.prompt: A friend asks for a login code that was texted to your phone. What should you do?
options: Send the code because friends are safe
Post the code in the group chat
Do not share the code and contact the friend another way
Try the code on several websites
standard.explanation: Login codes are account keys. A request for one may mean the friend's account was taken over.

standard.prompt: What is the best sign that a download deserves extra caution?
options: It promises free rewards and asks you to disable security settings
It comes from an official app store or vendor page
It has clear reviews from known sources
It explains what permissions it needs
standard.explanation: Requests to disable protections are a serious warning sign.

standard.prompt: A public Wi-Fi network at a cafe has no password. Which action is safest?
options: Use it for banking because it is convenient
Avoid sensitive tasks or use a trusted hotspot or VPN
Turn off updates to save data
Share the network password with everyone nearby
standard.explanation: Open networks are easier to monitor. Sensitive tasks should wait or use a safer connection.

standard.prompt: A quiz asks for your full name, school, birthday, and home address to reveal your 'cyber hero name.' What should you do?
options: Enter everything so the result is accurate
Give only the information you are comfortable sharing or skip it
Use a parent's payment card instead
Send the quiz to younger students
standard.explanation: Fun quizzes can collect personal data. You can decline or minimize what you share.

standard.prompt: Which message is most likely to be a scam?
options: Your teacher posts homework inside the official classroom app
A bank text asks you to move money to a 'safe account' right now
A parent reminds you to update your device tonight
A known store receipt arrives after a purchase you made
standard.explanation: Pressure to move money quickly is a classic scam pattern.

standard.prompt: What should you do after clicking a suspicious link by mistake?
options: Ignore it if nothing happens right away
Enter fake details to test the page
Close it, do not enter information, and report it to a trusted person
Share the link so others can inspect it
standard.explanation: Fast reporting and avoiding data entry reduce the risk.

standard.prompt: An app requests access to your contacts, camera, microphone, and location for a simple puzzle game. What is a safe response?
options: Accept all permissions because games need everything
Deny permissions that do not match the app's purpose
Give it your password instead
Turn off the lock screen
standard.explanation: Permissions should fit the app's real function.

standard.prompt: Which action helps a family recover faster after an account breach?
options: Keep recovery email and phone details current
Delete all messages without checking them
Use one shared password for every account
Avoid telling anyone
standard.explanation: Current recovery details make it easier to regain control safely.

standard.prompt: A caller says they are from tech support and asks to remote-control your device. What should you do?
options: Give control if they sound professional
Hang up and contact the company using its official support page
Read out all saved passwords
Install whatever they request
standard.explanation: Unexpected support calls are risky. Start a new conversation through official channels.

options: It makes passwords unnecessary everywhere
It adds another proof step if a password is stolen
It lets friends log in for you
It hides all online activity
standard.explanation: MFA helps block sign-ins that only have the password.

challenge.prompt: You receive a scholarship form from a lookalike domain that asks for your school login and a payment processing fee. What combination of clues should guide your response?
options: The scholarship topic and the low fee make it safe
The lookalike domain, credential request, and fee are enough to verify through official school channels
Any form with a logo is trustworthy
Payment requests are safe when they are small
challenge.explanation: Multiple risk signals together mean you should verify independently before sharing anything.

challenge.prompt: A marketplace buyer offers extra money if you continue the conversation off-platform and accept a reversible payment. What is the safest interpretation?
options: It may be an attempt to remove platform protections and create payment risk
Extra money proves the buyer is serious
Off-platform messages are always more private
Reversible payments cannot be abused
challenge.explanation: Moving off-platform and odd payment requests are common fraud patterns.

challenge.prompt: A family member's social account posts a strange investment link. What should you do before warning others?
options: Click the link to confirm every detail
Privately verify with the person through another channel and report the post if compromised
Comment your phone number under the post
Send your login code to help them recover
challenge.explanation: Verification through a separate channel avoids interacting with a compromised account.

challenge.prompt: A breach notice says your email and reused password were exposed. Which response is most complete?
options: Change only the breached account and keep the reused password elsewhere
Change the breached password, update every reused copy, enable MFA, and watch for suspicious activity
Wait for the company to fix every account
Delete your email address immediately
challenge.explanation: Password reuse means several accounts may be at risk, not just the breached service.
